title: Design Websites, Not Just Pages
body:
## Interactive Design: Design Sites, Not Pages

**Pages must serve the personality of a site, and each one is responsible for fulfilling its assigned objectives so that the site can grow like an intelligent organism.** Instead of wasting time worrying about each page as a separate unit, think of the site as a living organism. Your primary concern is the overall design; aside from being suitable for individual pages, it must appeal to that living, information-consuming entity that is a well-designed site. When you start designing a new site—even with the initial sketches—you should aim to create a design that resonates with users, aligns with the company’s goals, and conveys the intended message. It must have a consistent and cohesive look and feel, and it should be easy to expand in the future. Using a consistent page design throughout a site may often seem uncreative at first. You must persevere with it. The consistency of a site’s design is one of the factors that distinguishes amateur sites from professional ones. Sometimes, when working with pages related to the same topic, they fall victim to the tyranny of uniformity, conforming to a single design approach. And sometimes we designers think we would have treated them completely differently if they weren’t so closely related, even when dealing with the same topic. The designs may seem less daring or creative to us, but we’ve gained something very valuable: your site will have a uniform look across all pages, allowing users to immediately recognize it when they visit your site and, in turn, remember it when they see it in publications and magazines. Designing with this mindset also means your work is easier to revisit, both for you and for others. Don’t expect companies to always rely on the same person to work on their website—most of the time spent on a site is dedicated to its maintenance. The goal of good design isn’t just a successful launch but also effective maintenance. The difference between website creators and maintainers lies precisely in this—or do you intend to remain the maintainer of every site you create? This, of course, does not rule out any commercial ventures involving separate investments in design, illustration, animations, etc.—new interactive proposals, and of course new investments… but that’s another story, other projects. Juan M. Estévez Artistic Director at Linkz Multimedia
